nativeRange.habitat: Forested streams and tributaries with substrates of sand, mud, leaf litter, and fallen branches; slow-moving shallow water with dense riparian cover

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (essential for barbel health)
leaf litter (dried Indian almond or oak leaves)
driftwood and twisted roots for shelter
dense planting with Java fern, Anubias, or amazon sword

diet.notes: Opportunistic benthic forager accepting a wide range of sinking foods: high-quality micro-pellets, sinking wafers, frozen or live bloodworm, Tubifex, brine shrimp, and daphnia. Cannot subsist on tank scraps alone — dedicated feeding is required. Will also pick at soft algae and biofilm. Feed once or twice daily, targeting the substrate after lights-out to reduce competition.

avoidWith: large boisterous species that outcompete at feeding
Oscar and other large South American cichlids

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo of Corydoras elegans (Gastrodermus elegans); typical lateral view showing reticulated dorsal pattern.

summary: The Elegant Corydoras is a small Amazonian armored catfish, reaching around 4 to 5.5 cm, with subtly reticulated markings that set it apart from more boldly patterned relatives. Unusually for the genus, it tends to form loose shoals in mid-water rather than staying on the bottom. It is a peaceful, undemanding species best kept in groups of six or more.
careNotes:
The Elegant Corydoras (Corydoras elegans) is a small, peaceful catfish from the western Amazon basin — primarily Peruvian rivers including the Ríos Yavarí, Itaya, Nanay, and Ucayali, extending into Ecuador (Ríos Aguarico, Napo) and Brazil (rio Solimões). It reaches roughly 4–5.5 cm standard length, making it a genuinely small species suitable for well-maintained tanks from about 60 L with a 60 cm footprint.

Unlike most corydoras, which are strict bottom dwellers, C. elegans frequently forms loose shoals in mid-water — a behaviour unusual enough in the genus to be highlighted by specialists. A group of six or more is strongly recommended both to encourage this natural shoaling behaviour and to reduce individual stress. The swim zone is effectively "all" layers, though benthic foraging remains important.

Fine sand is strongly preferred; smooth rounded gravel is an acceptable second choice if kept meticulously clean. Coarse or sharp gravel can abrade the sensitive barbels over time. Provide leaf litter, driftwood, and plant cover to replicate the shaded forest-stream biotope. Water should be soft to moderately hard (gH 2–15, kH 1–8) and slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.5); temperature 20–26 °C suits the cooler western-Amazon origin.

Like all callichthyid catfishes, C. elegans is a facultative air-breather and will periodically dash to the surface to gulp air — this is normal. The sharp pectoral-fin locking spines can inflict a painful wound and carry mild venom; handle with care when netting.

A note on taxonomy: a 2024 phylogenomic revision of the family Callichthyidae resurrected the genus Gastrodermus Cope 1878, originally established for this very species, placing C. elegans as Gastrodermus elegans. As of 2026 the trade continues to use Corydoras elegans; both names appear in the literature.
breedingNotes:
Breeding is achievable in captivity with modest effort. Condition a group of two or three males per female with varied high-protein live and frozen foods. Spawning is typically triggered by large (50–70%) water changes with slightly cooler water (2–3 °C below tank temperature) combined with increased surface agitation and oxygenation. Repeat daily until spawning occurs — pairs perform the classic corydoras T-position, with the female holding 2–4 eggs between cupped pelvic fins while the male fertilises them. Eggs are then pressed onto the glass, plant leaves, or other firm surfaces.

Eggs hatch in 3–4 days at 24 °C. Once yolk sacs are fully absorbed (another 2–3 days), fry accept microworm and Artemia nauplii. Maintain fry over a thin layer of fine sand to reduce pathogen load. Breedability is rated easy to moderate for an experienced aquarist.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Corydoras elegans
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Peru — Ríos Yavarí, Itaya, Nanay, Carahuayte, Tamaya, Ampiyacu, Ucayali; Ecuador — Ríos Aguarico, Napo; Brazil — rio Solimões), adult size (45–55 mm), temperature (20–26 °C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness (18–268 ppm), habitat description, mid-water shoaling behaviour, diet notes, breeding trigger protocol, egg incubation time (3–4 days), fry foods, and venomous-spine caution.

additional.name: FishBase — Gastrodermus elegans (Corydoras elegans)
additional.notes: Cross-checked max standard length (5.1 cm SL), temperature (22–26 °C), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (2–25 dH), taxonomy (Callichthyidae, Siluriformes), native distribution (Upper Amazon basin, South America). Now lists species as Gastrodermus elegans following the 2024 revision.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Cory Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: No dedicated C. elegans page; general corydoras guide supplemented substrate guidance (smooth sand preferred), feeding advice (dedicated sinking foods required, not algae scrapers), group size (six or more), compatibility notes, and breeding conditioning overview. Noted that mild venom is associated with sharp pectoral-fin spines.

additional.name: Aquarium Glaser — Corydoras Revised: New Genus Names (2024 Dias/Britto)
additional.notes: Confirms the 2024 Dias, Britto et al. phylogenomic revision of Corydoradinae. Corydoras elegans is placed in the resurrected genus Gastrodermus Cope 1878, as Gastrodermus elegans — C. elegans is the type species for Gastrodermus. Genus Hoplisoma covers a different lineage (lineage 9, 87+ species); elegans group belongs to Gastrodermus (lineage 5).

additional.name: AMAZONAS Magazine — Overhauled: A Massive Revision of the Genus Corydoras
additional.notes: Secondary overview of the 2024 Dias et al. revision. Confirms seven resurrected/new genera within Corydoradinae; provides hobbyist context for the reclassification impact on trade names.

fish.breedingNotes: Spawning is triggered by large water changes using slightly cooler water, combined with increased surface agitation. A ratio of two or three males per female is recommended. Pairs engage in the T-position typical of corydoras, with eggs pressed onto glass or plant surfaces afterward. Incubation takes around 3 to 4 days, after which fry accept microworm and Artemia nauplii.
